misconceptionSummary: Students calculate missing sides by guessing rather than using known lengths and shape properties.

lines.text: Student guesses: missing sides = 3 cm each  ✗
Missing sides can be calculated from given lengths

steps.narration: On compound shapes, unlabelled sides are not unknown — they can be calculated from the sides that are labelled. Guessing random values gives a wrong perimeter.

visuals.ruleText: On a rectilinear shape, opposite parallel sides add to the same total in each direction
visuals.subText: Missing horizontal side = total width − known horizontal side

steps.narration: For L-shapes and other rectilinear shapes, the unlabelled sides can be found by subtraction. The total horizontal extent equals the sum of all horizontal segments — so the missing one is the total minus the known ones.

lines.text: Overall width = 9 cm. Known horizontal = 5 cm
Overall height = 7 cm. Known vertical = 3 cm

steps.narration: Find each missing side by subtracting: overall width nine minus known width five gives four centimetres. Overall height seven minus known height three gives four centimetres. Now all sides are known.

visuals.expression: Missing side = overall span − known side
visuals.label: Use parallel-side subtraction — never guess missing lengths

steps.narration: Calculate missing sides before summing — never guess. The parallel-side rule gives exact values from the information already on the diagram.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: guessing unlabelled sides on an L-shape. Use the overall width and height: missing side = overall span − known sides in that direction.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Unlabelled sides on rectilinear shapes are never truly unknown. Subtract the known sides from the overall span in each direction to find the exact missing lengths.
